title: Astoria Requiem
text:
This website is about the piece Astoria Requiem, a commissioned piece for Friends After Good Sound. We will share information, and video about this piece as it evolves.
Astoria Requiem will be performed live at Performance Works NW on September 12, 2026.

title: Astoria Requiem
text:
Background



24-foot cannery fishing boats, with only sails and a rower to power them. Image courtesy of the Clatsop County Historical Society



A few weeks of warm weather sent the snows into the Columbia in torrents and the great river rushed down to the ocean, swollen and turbulent. Great tracts were inundated and the tides affected (p 41)


To pull their heavy 24-foot boats against such a current was a feat few of them were capable of, and the only course open to the majority was to face death with fortitude. (Salmon Fever, River’s End)

Astoria and the Columbia River Bar is a dangerous place, where two unstoppable forces meet and make for unpredictable weather and conditions.



Cannery Boat - image from Chinook Observer


In the late 19th century, many salmon canneries in Astoria needed fishermen to supply them. These fishermen (mostly in pairs) would go out in 24 foot boats, floating out to the ocean on the ebbtide, harvest the fish from the gill nets, catch fish, and float back into Astoria with the incoming flood tide.
It was a dangerous profession, especially because of the lack of engines on the boat made the fishermen especially vulnerable to to the elements.



Postcard showing the salmon fishing fleet in the 1910s


In early May of 1880, a sudden gale caught many of these boats by surprise, and resulted in the death of over 25 fishermen.[^1] This piece is an attempt to memorialize the fishermen, and not forget them.

title: Astoria Requiem
text:
Structure of the Piece
This piece is in 3 movements:

Two Forces, a short intro introducing the two forces of the Columbia River Bar
Ebbtide and Storm, featuring a storm using shoegazer elements.
Requiem, in memory of those lost at sea

title: Astoria Requiem
text:
Land Acknowledgement
We recognize that the land of Astoria and the Lower Columbia was inhabited by tribes including the Chinook, Clatsop, Kathlamet, Wahkiakum, Multnomah, Cascades, Tualatin Kalapuya, Molalla, Wasco, Clackamas, Cowlitz, Skilloot and Atfalati have ancestral connections to the lower Columbia and continue to be stewards of the river. We recognize that tribal territories were often shared and overlapping, and that this may be an incomplete list of those who lived on these lands.
The Chinook and Clatsop tribes (with others) are working to be recognized as the Chinook Indian Nation. Learn more and donate at https://chinooknation.org/
[^1] Some sources (such asPacific Graveyard) say that over 200 fishermen perished in the storm, but an investigative article by the Chinook Observer suggests that the loss was not that large, nor does the editor of Salmon Fever, River’s Edge agree with the larger number.

title: Part 2: Ebbtide and Storm
text:
Tonquin crossing - from Oregon Historical Society



Mr Ackland reports seeing two men on the bottom of a boat, lead colored bottom, nearing the breakers yesterday morning. He could do nothing to save the men, and they bid him farewell by tipping their hats as they entered the jaws of death. - (Daily Astorian, p 34)


They saw the cruel wave rear its crested head, and drawing the now helpless man in with the undertow, it lifted him up and then broke over him. After this they saw him no more, and it is probable that this is the last of Louis C. Webber until “the sea shall give up the dead that are in it.” - The Daily Astorian, May 30, 1880

title: Part 2: Ebbtide and Storm
text:
Tonquin crossing - from Oregon Historical Society



Mr Ackland reports seeing two men on the bottom of a boat, lead colored bottom, nearing the breakers yesterday morning. He could do nothing to save the men, and they bid him farewell by tipping their hats as they entered the jaws of death. - (Daily Astorian, p 34)


They saw the cruel wave rear its crested head, and drawing the now helpless man in with the undertow, it lifted him up and then broke over him. After this they saw him no more, and it is probable that this is the last of Louis C. Webber until “the sea shall give up the dead that are in it.” - The Daily Astorian, May 30, 1880

title: Part 2: Ebbtide and Storm
text:
The Music
This section starts with the ebbtide, where the boats drift in harmony with the tide. The floodtide is when the disaster comes, fighting with the current with the breaker waves sweeping over the tiny fishing boats.
The trio is going to be subjected to tremendous forces, and their job is to weather the storm. The “storm” will be audio from a synthesizer and samplers driven by a generative sequence, which will provide “breaker waves” (detuning) that are surprise events (dissonances) that the quartet needs to react to. The detuning will move the tonal center around, disorienting
The accordion acts like the boat puller, providing the rhythm of the ship.
The four voices of the quartet are fisherpeople lost in the storm - some boats will make it, and some do not. Each voice will have a motif, or a variation of a motif.

title: Part 3: Requiem
text:
Following a storm, many bodies could not be immediately identified. As a result, local papers would publish what the deceased were wearing, and the contents in their pockets. For example, a compelling story could be derived from this passage:

Said body wore one white shirt, one coarse grey woolen shirt, one pair coarse cotton drawers, one pair blue overalls, 1 pair duck overalls, black vest, faded black beaver coat, southwester and oil skin coat. […] In pocket were found some white rags, a red silk handkerchief with J. Richten marked in one corner, a copy of the DAILY ASTORIAN of April 22, a ring with a small key, a white handled 3 bladed pocket knife, a pocket comb, a beer ticket from Ginder, a title table for April, half doller US coin, I Bavarian 3 kreutzer piece of 1851 vintage, a cannery ticket marked A. Booth, No. 6, April 22nd, Boat No. 2, No. of fish 4. - Contents of an unknown body found (Daily Astorian)

This movement is inspired by such entries.

title: Part 3: Requiem
text:
About the Movement
The last section is an elegaic one, drifting and lost. The river and ocean take lives away, and we are subject to the aftermath. This section is simpler and have music that expands as it goes as the instruments are processed using granular synthesis.

title: Part 3: Requiem
text:
The Lost Fishermen
Fishermen lost during 1890 (From Salmon Fever, River’s End p. 40):

Thomas Gill and George Adams, G. W. Hume’s boat No. 11.
Mr. Gadway and net tender, fished for Astoria Fishery or large Kinney cannery.
Peter J. Blegan and boat puller, Independent.
Andrew Hansen, fished for G. W. Hume
Henry Heinsen, died May 4 in a boat on Chinook Beach after drifting about in a swamped boat eight hours.
Agust Wrentz and Dick Jones, fished for J. G. Megler and Company. Wrentz found entangled in net in boot in the Chinook River.
Charles Harvey, found on May 12th. Coroners inquest on the 13th. Fished for J. G. Megler & Co. with a man named Pike.
Alex Pholson, drowned May 3rd or 4th. Fished for the Anglo American packing company with Mr. Johnson, boat No 23.
Andrew Gill and Alex Chase, have not the date.

title: Part 1: Two Forces
text:
Image from Casey Lee Swayze
Part of the reason the mouth of the Columbia is so dangerous is that it localizes two opposing forces: that of the Columbia, which is channeled and strong (it has no delta to disperse its force), and the tidal forces. When the two are in opposition, fierce gales can erupt, making it dangerous for fishermen.

title: Part 1: Two Forces
text:
First Nation Perspective
It is unfair to talk about a tragedy along the Columbia River without talking about the Chinook Nation and their respect and stewardship of the Lower Columbia River. They were here long before us, and had respect for the river.

So, you had no Columbia River at all. And the people over there, their food sources were limited, they didn’t have enough food all the time. And Coyote knew if there was a way, he could create a channel or path for the salmon to come from the ocean to the East side of the mountains, that that would help the people. - All tribes Eastern Washington creation story


The first to come forward was Salmon. He gave the humans his body for food. The second to give a gift was Water. She promised to be the home to the salmon. After that, everyone else gave the humans a gift, but it was special that the first to give their gifts were Salmon and Water. - Spirit of the Salmon, Creation Story


We All Pull and Support Each Other. Nothing occurs in isolation. In a family of the canoe, we are ready for whatever comes. The family can argue, mock, ignore each other, at its worst, but that family will never let itself sink. - Ten Rules of the Canoe, Chinook Nation

title: Part 1: Two Forces
text:
The music
This movement contains two main motifs:

Motif 1: The force of the columbia (higher range) - shorter scale (Viola)
Motif 2: The tidal forces (lower range) - longer scale (Cello and Piano)
Backing: Guitar
